entities.display: GOLC
GOLC v1
Offline Foundation and Delivery Traceability

A contributor can discover toolchain versions, setup, generation, validation, build, test, packaging, application-default, and runtime-configuration entrypoints from one documented root project configuration.
Project configuration is separated into independently validatable concerns without duplicating authoritative values across files.
A contributor and CI can invoke the same documented project commands for generation, validation, build, test, and packaging.
Secrets and machine-local values remain outside committed project configuration and are represented by documented names and safe examples.
Every milestone, phase, requirement, plan, and executable task has a durable local identifier that remains usable offline.
The repository maintains a credential-free mapping from durable local identifiers to immutable Linear UUIDs without making Linear the only source of planning truth.
A contributor can preview and run an idempotent reconciliation that creates or updates the intended Linear project, milestones, issues, and sub-issues without duplicating retried work.
Linear synchronization reports ambiguity, partial GraphQL errors, pagination, and rate limiting without blocking local planning, builds, tests, or application runtime.

Modular Fixtures and Deployments
A user can load fixture definitions authored in a documented, versioned YAML schema.
The application rejects duplicate keys, ambiguous YAML constructs, invalid ranges, and unsupported fixture semantics with actionable diagnostics.
A user can import an Open Fixture Library definition through GOLC's canonical validation and normalization pipeline.
A user can create, edit, validate, and share a custom YAML fixture definition.
A show pins each fixture definition by stable identity, schema version, content revision, and hash so library updates cannot silently change an existing show.
A user can inspect a fixture definition's source, provenance, validation result, and unsupported or lossy import details before using it.
A show author can define a logical pool of compatible fixtures independently of the concrete fixture count, addresses, and deployment hardware.
A show author can create a deployment that maps logical pools to concrete fixture instances, modes, universes, and addresses.
A show author can add or remove fixtures from a pool and receive an impact review covering all dependent groups, themes, palettes, scenes, chases, motion presets, and controller mappings.
A show author can configure propagation behavior for each pool update while review-before-apply remains the default.
A reviewed pool update is applied atomically so dependents never observe a partially updated deployment.
A show author can replace a fixture model by mapping shared semantic capabilities rather than raw channel positions.
A fixture replacement review identifies missing, incompatible, and unsupported capabilities and never silently approximates them.
A show author can accept, revise, or cancel a pool or fixture-substitution impact plan before it changes the show.
